danger_signals.visual_form: Unused startup screen text: 'WARNING!——BEWARE; DO NOT TEST THE ORACLE THIS IS NOT A GAME'
danger_signals.meaning: Communicates that the application transcends entertainment; carries genuine supernatural/occult danger. Meta-signal to player: treat this seriously.

danger_signals.visual_form: Unused graphics asset: single droplet of blood for title screen
danger_signals.meaning: Supernatural harm implied; suggests consequences beyond normal game failure states.

danger_signals.visual_form: Dual content warnings (unique to NES): 'Ages 14+' and 'Entertainment Purposes Only'
danger_signals.meaning: Visual/textual signal of prohibited/dangerous content (nudity, religious imagery). Warns that material has real-world implications.

safety_signals.visual_form: ™ and © symbols on title screens (revisions differ)
safety_signals.meaning: Legal/commercial framing; legitimizes the application as official product, not rogue occult tool. Provides safety through institutional authority.
Rational framing that contradicts the 'genuine oracle' threat. Signals safe, bounded experience despite occult theme.

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: 78-card deck (Major and Minor Arcana); Celtic Cross layout
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Card shuffle animation; tarot card imagery; generated readings displayed on screen

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Trademark symbols (™, ©); publisher logos (Tradewest); instruction booklet with historical tarot origin explanations
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Official branding; legal disclaimers; educational framing of card meanings and positions

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Blood imagery; warning text; religious and nude content
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Unused warning graphics suggesting earlier, darker framing; urban legend association (deaths predicted by readings)

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Game Framed as Legitimate Entertainment Tool
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Title screen with trademark symbols; instruction booklet with tarot history; age warnings as content labels rather than supernatural warnings
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game accepts player input (name, birth date, gender, question) and processes systematically, generating readings via Celtic Cross layout—simulates rational divination software

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Game Perceived as Genuine Occult Oracle
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Unused warning text ('DO NOT TEST THE ORACLE'); blood droplet asset; double-warning structure (age + entertainment disclaimer); religious/nude imagery
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Urban legend emerges: players report accurate death predictions. Game generates readings that feel personally significant, blurring simulation boundary.

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Game as Liminal Space Between Rational and Supernatural
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dual framings coexist: commercial disclaimers + occult imagery + unused darker assets suggesting suppressed original intent
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: No actual game mechanics—only repeated readings. Player agency limited to input questions; output generated by system beyond player control, fostering passive acceptance of 'fate'

environmental_storytelling.example: The instruction booklet provides tarot history and card meanings, treating the oracle as real object with documented past. Warnings frame it not as fiction but as dangerous reality requiring context.

The unused warning screen ('BEWARE; DO NOT TEST THE ORACLE') and blood droplet suggest developer awareness of genuine supernatural danger. Their removal signals censorship or compromise—world-state implies original intent was darker.

Rev 0 to Rev 1 changes (trademark placement, subtitle addition) visually document the process of making the oracle seem more official/safe. Earlier version more ambiguous; revision adds commercial legitimacy.

Nintendo's unusual approval of nudity and religious imagery (typically prohibited) signals this title exists in special category—dangerous enough to require explicit warnings, legitimate enough to sell.

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Automatically shuffles cards; generates readings via established layout; produces personalized output based on player identity (name, birth date, gender). Behavior suggests autonomous intelligence responding to input.

Dual behavior: functions as entertainment software (processes input rationally) AND as supernatural oracle (players report uncanny accuracy, game becomes subject of urban legend). System behavior unchanged; interpretation by players creates the danger signal.
Inputs personal data (name, birth date, gender, question), suggesting vulnerability/openness. Passive reception of output creates psychological investment in 'accuracy' regardless of randomness.

notes: Taboo is a unique manifestation study: it is fundamentally a non-game (no win/loss, mechanics, or progression) that communicates world-state primarily through paratextual elements (warnings, disclaimers, instruction booklet) and unused/suppressed assets. The danger and faction signals emerge from the gap between official framing (entertainment software) and alternative interpretation (genuine occult tool). Visual communication is minimal (card imagery, text) but psychological impact is high due to the subject matter's real-world cultural associations. The game's power lies in ambiguity: warnings intended to create safety paradoxically reinforce the occult threat narrative. Unused graphics reveal developer intent toward darker original framing, suggesting institutional pressure (Nintendo censorship) shaped the final manifestation. Urban legend status indicates the game successfully communicated a 'dangerous world-state' despite—or because of—official disclaimers.
